title: practical english usage - Part 87
content:
- Singing is forbidden.

People are not allowed/permitted to smoke in the lecture room.

- Smoking is not allowed/permitted in the lecture room.

Note the corresponding passive structures.

The headmistress has forbidden children to sing.. .

- The headmistress has forbidden singing in the corridors.

We don't allow/permit people to smoke in the lecture room.

- We don't allow/permit smoking in the lecture room.

I wouldn't advise you to take the car...

- I wouldn't advise taking the car - there's nowhere to park.

If there is an object we  normally use an infinitive. Compare: In active clauses after these verbs, we  use an -ing form if there is no object.

4 advise,       allow,      permit        and   forbid -ing                                                         Section 9 Infinitives,        forms     and   Past   Participles       after    Nouns,    Verbs,   etc grammar •  105  infinitives or -ing forms: both possible with different uses For details of structures with to + -ing, ►     I012.

to strangers.

I'm not accustomed to giving/give personal information about myself / intend telling / to tell her what I think.

infinitive without much difference of meaning.

After these words and expressions we  can generally use either an -ing form or an committed            to 11   attempt,         intend,       continue,         can't     bear,     be   accustomed             to,   be (xor. .. started realising...) He started to realise that if you wanted to eat you had to work.

I slowly began to understand how she felt, (     .. .-began-undcrstanding...) Infinitives are also preferred with understand, realise and know.

I'm beginning to learn karate, (     Pm  beginning learning karate:) After progressive forms of begin and start, infinitives are preferred.

He started talking / to talk about golf, and everybody went out of the room.

She began playing / to play the guitar when she was six.

important difference.

Begin and start can be followed by infinitives or -ing forms. Usually there is no 10 begin      and   start For more about like, ►    514.   For details of structures with prefer, ►    560.

Would you like to dance? (= Do  you want to dance now?) Do you like dancing? (= Do  you enjoy dancing?) Compare: 'Can I give you a lift?' 'No thanks, I'd prefer to walk.' not I'd like to tell you something, (     J'd-like tellingyowsomething.) often used.

After would like, would prefer, would hate and would love, infinitives are most I hate to tell you this, but we're going to miss the train.

Hate + infinitive can be used to introduce unwelcome news.

When 1 pour tea I like to put the milk in first. (Like =  'choose': infinitive I like climbing / to climb mountains. (Like = 'enjoy': both forms possible) Like + infinitive is used to talk about choices and habits. Compare: I don't get up on Sundays. I prefer staying / to stay in bed.

/ hate working / to work at weekends.

a great difference of meaning.

After these four verbs, both infinitives and -ing forms can often be used without 9 like,    love,     hate     and   prefer I taught myself to code.

in Germany.

She learned to read German at school, but she learned to speak it successfully learning a skill.

Infinitives are preferred when we  talk about the result of the study - about grammar •  105  infinitives or -ing forms: both possible with different uses I'm interested in finding out / to find out what she did with all that money.

interested + infinitive are common.

To  talk about a wish to find out something, both interested in + -ing and I shall be interested to see how long it lasts.

I'm interested to see that Alice and Jake are going out together.

to whales.

I was interested to read in the paper that scientists have found out how to talk used.

To  talk about reactions to things one learns, interested + infinitive is commonly 16 interested will win.') Kroftova's sure to win - the other girl hasn't got a chance. (= 'ɪ am sure she eerlain-ef-e^ling-. ..) not The repairs are certain to cost more than you think, (     The repairs are Certain!sure + infinitive refer to the speaker's or writer's own feelings.
